title: Negative-origin (secondary-monitor) iTerm window crashes get_iterm_window_bounds() with uncaught ValueError, breaking its graceful-return contract for all callers
line: 22-23, 69 (parse called outside the try block at 25-32); also images.py:39-40
description: get_iterm_window_bounds() is documented and used everywhere as a soft probe that returns WindowBoundsResult(available=False, ...) on failure (see adapt.py:32-37, status.py:105, live_stage.py:189, watch_live.py:172). But _raw_iterm_window_bounds_points() builds Region.parse(raw.removeprefix("OK|")) at line 69, and Region.__post_init__ (images.py:39-40) raises ValueError when x<0 or y<0. AppleScript 'bounds of window 1' returns NEGATIVE left/top whenever the iTerm window is on a secondary monitor placed to the left of, or above, the main display (a very common multi-monitor layout). The Region.parse call sits in _raw_iterm_window_bounds_points(), which get_iterm_window_bounds() invokes at line 22-23 OUTSIDE its own try/except (the try only starts at line 25 and wraps just the pixel conversion). So the ValueError propagates uncaught out of get_iterm_window_bounds(). In run_live_stage (live_stage.py:189) this fires AFTER the Safari and iTerm windows have already been opened, aborting the whole capture, writing no report, and leaving the staged GUI windows open. Reproduction (mocking the osascript subprocess to simulate a left-monitor window): patching subprocess.run to return stdout 'OK|-1440,300,980,760' makes get_iterm_window_bounds() raise 'ValueError: region x/y must be non-negative' instead of returning a result. Region.parse('-1440,300,980,760') alone also raises the same ValueError.
fix: Wrap the Region.parse call in _raw_iterm_window_bounds_points() (or the whole _raw_iterm_window_bounds_points() invocation at line 22) in a try/except that converts ValueError into WindowBoundsResult(available=False, region=None, message=...). Better: stop rejecting negative origins for window bounds entirely — a window legitimately has a negative on-screen origin on multi-monitor setups. Either use a separate bounds type that permits negative x/y, or translate the negative origin into the screenshot coordinate space (where _points_region_to_screenshot_pixels already clamps to max(0, x)) before constructing the Region.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against current HEAD code. Region.__post_init__ (images.py:38-40) raises ValueError when x<0 or y<0. _raw_iterm_window_bounds_points() builds Region.parse(raw.removeprefix("OK|")) at iterm_window.py:69, and get_iterm_window_bounds() invokes _raw_iterm_window_bounds_points() at line 22 OUTSIDE its try/except (the try starts at line 25 and wraps only _points_region_to_screenshot_pixels). I reproduced both: (1) Region.parse('-1440,300,980,760') raises 'ValueError: region x/y must be non-negative'; (2) mocking subprocess.run to return stdout 'OK|-1440,300,980,760' makes get_iterm_window_bounds() raise an UNCAUGHT ValueError instead of returning WindowBoundsResult(available=False,...). The reproduction output matches the claim exactly (not fabricated). All callers (adapt.py:32, status.py:105, live_stage.py:189, watch_live.py:172, cli.py:1241) branch on result.available and rely on the graceful-return contract; none wrap the call in try/except, so the ValueError propagates. At live_stage.py:189 the call occurs after capture_screen, so it aborts the capture flow as described. No test covers the negative-origin iTerm case, and git history for iterm_window.py (af8a318, fa05c58) does not address it, so this is not fixed in prior rounds. AppleScript 'bounds of window 1' genuinely returns negative left/top for iTerm windows on a left/above secondary monitor, so the trigger is real macOS behavior, not contrived. Severity: it breaks the documented soft-probe contract on a common multi-monitor layout, but only under that specific condition rather than universally — HIGH is defensible (borderline MEDIUM), so I keep HIGH.

title: iTerm window-bounds-to-pixels scaling is wrong on multi-monitor because screencapture -x captures only the main display while Finder desktop bounds span all displays
description: _points_region_to_screenshot_pixels derives scale factors as screenshot_pixels / desktop_points. _screenshot_size_pixels() calls capture_screen, which runs `screencapture -x <file>` (screenshot.py:28-34) with no -D/display flag — on macOS that captures ONLY the main display. But _desktop_bounds_points() reads Finder 'bounds of window of desktop', which on a multi-monitor system returns the bounding rectangle of the ENTIRE desktop spanning all displays (point_width/point_height can far exceed the main display, and left/top can be negative). The two measurements are over different coordinate extents, so scale_x/scale_y are computed against mismatched denominators, mis-scaling the region. When the iTerm window is on a non-main monitor, the mapped origin also lands outside the single-display screenshot, and the subsequent clamp_to (images.py:55-59) raises 'region origin ... is outside image' — another path that surfaces as a failure during run_live_stage capture. The math is only correct on a single-display setup where Finder desktop bounds equal the captured display.
fix: Make the two measurements consistent for the targeted display: either capture all displays / the specific display that holds the iTerm window (screencapture -D <n> or capture per-display) and use that display's point bounds, or query the actual display geometry (e.g. via the display containing the window) instead of Finder's full-desktop bounds. Document that the current scaling only supports a single-display layout and fail with a clear message when multiple displays are detected, so callers fall back to an explicit --region.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against real code. _points_region_to_screenshot_pixels (iterm_window.py:105-118) derives scale_x/scale_y = screenshot_pixels / desktop_points. The two denominators come from different coordinate extents:

1. _screenshot_size_pixels() (95-102) calls capture_screen -> screencapture.py:28-34 runs `screencapture -x <file>` with NO -D/display flag. Confirmed via `man screencapture`: `-D <display> ... 1 is main, 2 secondary`. The well-documented macOS behavior of a single-file capture with no -D is main-display-only. To capture multiple displays you must pass multiple file args.
2. _desktop_bounds_points() (75-92) reads Finder `bounds of window of desktop`, which returns the union rectangle spanning ALL displays on multi-monitor (point_width/height can far exceed the main display).

Reproduced the single-display correctness: on this Retina machine _desktop_bounds_points() = (0,0,1470,956) and the screenshot is 2940x1912, giving scale_x=scale_y=2.0 (exactly the retina factor) — correct. This confirms the claim's assertion that the math is only correct when Finder desktop bounds equal the captured display.

On multi-monitor (e.g. main 1470pt wide + secondary extending desktop to 3470pt wide), screenshot_width stays 2940 (main only) while point_width becomes 3470, so scale_x ≈ 0.847 instead of 2.0 — x and width are badly mis-scaled. If the iTerm window is on a non-main monitor, the mapped origin can land beyond the 2940px main-only screenshot and crop_image -> Region.clamp_to (images.py:55-59) raises "region origin ... is outside image", surfacing during run_live_stage capture (live_stage.py:186-192). (Note: the negative-origin sub-claim is wrong — max(0,x) at line 118 plus Region.__post_init__ clamp non-negative — but the dominant failure modes, silent mis-scaling and the outside-image raise, are accurate.)

Grepped the whole package: no -D usage, no display/monitor count detection, no guard anywhere. Bug is unmitigated. A manual --region fallback exists but the default path silently samples the wrong screen region (wrong contrast measurement) on any multi-monitor setup.

title: _prune_backups crashes with uncaught FileNotFoundError when a concurrent writer prunes the same backup dir (stat-after-glob race)
description: backup_file() -> _prune_backups() builds `sorted(source.parent.glob(...), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime)`. The `p.stat()` call is NOT wrapped in any error handling (unlike the sibling _prune_artifacts in watch_live.py:124-126 which guards its sorted/stat with try/except OSError). When two writers target the same path concurrently (e.g. two `term-chameleon apply-mode` runs on the same Dynamic Profile JSON, or `setup` install racing a manual `apply`, or repeated rapid invocations), thread/process A globs a backup file, then thread/process B's own _prune_backups unlinks it, then A calls .stat() on the now-deleted path and raises FileNotFoundError. This propagates uncaught out of backup_file and aborts the entire apply/install/uninstall/fixes operation (modes.py:37, install.py:49/107, watch_daemon.py:239/284, fixes.py:83). Reproduction (44-53% failure rate with 8 threads x 30 calls): run `_prune_backups`/`backup_file` from multiple threads against one source file — confirmed FileNotFoundError traceback at safe_io.py:30. The existing test test_backup_file_prune_tolerates_already_deleted_backups (test_safe_io.py:166) only deletes a backup BEFORE the call (so glob never returns it) and does NOT exercise the glob-then-stat window, so this gap is uncovered.
fix: Wrap the sorted(glob, key=stat) in try/except OSError returning early on failure, mirroring _prune_artifacts in watch_live.py:124-126. Better: use a stat key that tolerates races, e.g. `key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime if p.exists() else 0` is still racy; the robust fix is to collect (path, mtime) pairs inside a per-file try/except that skips vanished files, then sort the survivors.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against real code and reproduced. safe_io.py:28-31 builds `sorted(source.parent.glob(...), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime)` with NO error handling, while the sibling _prune_artifacts at watch_live.py:124-127 wraps the identical `sorted(glob, key=stat)` pattern in try/except OSError — the asymmetry the claim describes is exactly correct. The p.stat() inside the sort key runs on a path that glob already returned; if a concurrent writer's _prune_backups unlinks it first, .stat() raises FileNotFoundError (an OSError subclass). The contextlib.suppress(OSError) at line 33-34 only guards old.unlink(), not the stat-during-sort.

Reproduction: an 8-thread x 30-call script against one source file (via .venv/bin/python) failed 7/8 threads on every one of 5 runs. Traceback confirms FileNotFoundError at safe_io.py:30 (`key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime`) propagating out of backup_file at line 43 — matching the cited evidence precisely. The claim's stated 44-53% per-call failure rate is unverified exactly but the qualitative reproduction is solid.

Test gap confirmed: test_backup_file_prune_tolerates_already_deleted_backups (test_safe_io.py:166) calls bp.unlink() BEFORE backup_file(), so the deleted file is never returned by glob — it does not exercise the glob-then-stat window. So the race path is genuinely uncovered.

Call sites confirmed: fixes.py:83, modes.py:37, install.py:49/107, watch_daemon.py:239/284. An uncaught exception there aborts the apply/install/fixes/daemon operation.

Severity adjusted from HIGH to MEDIUM. The crash and asymmetry are real, but realistic exploitation requires genuine concurrency: two simultaneous CLI invocations on the same Dynamic Profile, or the watch daemon racing a manual command. These are single-user CLI/daemon tools, not a multi-writer server; concurrent same-file writes are an edge case rather than a routine path. Impact is an aborted operation (the failed writer's new backup was already copied at line 42 before the prune), not data loss or corruption — pruning is explicitly best-effort. The fix is a trivial, correct hardening that should mirror _prune_artifacts.

title: watch-live PID file is leaked (stale) on SIGTERM/SIGKILL/crash because cleanup is only registered via atexit, which does not run on signals
description: _setup_pid_ownership registers PID-file removal with atexit.register(_remove_pid). atexit handlers run on normal interpreter exit and on SIGINT (which raises KeyboardInterrupt), but NOT on SIGTERM, SIGKILL, or a hard crash. SIGTERM is the normal way launchd/the OS/`kill`/system-shutdown terminates a background daemon. So the documented promise in watch_live.py:158-160 and watch_daemon.py:206-208 ('removed via atexit when the process exits, so the duplicate-launch guard ... does not mistake a recycled PID for a live watcher' / 'prevents stale PID files when the watcher is killed or crashes') is false for the most common termination paths. After SIGTERM the PID file persists pointing at a dead PID; if the OS later recycles that PID to an unrelated live process, the AutoLaunch guard's os.kill(pid,0) returns True and the watcher NEVER restarts (wedged daemon). No signal module is imported anywhere in src/. Reproduction: fork a child that registers atexit PID-cleanup, SIGTERM it -> PID file still present afterward (confirmed).
fix: Install a SIGTERM (and SIGHUP) handler in run_watch_live that removes the PID file and re-raises/exits, so cleanup runs on the normal kill path. For the recycled-PID case, also record a start-time or a unique token alongside the PID and verify it on the guard side, or have the guard treat a PID whose cmdline does not match the watcher as not-running.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against real code and reproduced both technical premises.

CODE: src/term_chameleon/watch_live.py:140-162 — _setup_pid_ownership writes the PID then registers removal ONLY via atexit.register(_remove_pid). No signal handler is installed anywhere in src/ (grep for 'signal' in src/ hits only a comment in watch_daemon.py:60; no `import signal`, no SIGTERM/SIGHUP handler). The docstring at lines 143-146 and watch_daemon.py:206-208 explicitly promise the PID file is removed "when the process exits" / "prevents stale PID files when the watcher is killed or crashes".

REPRODUCTION (via .venv/bin/python): A child that registers atexit PID-cleanup and sleeps, sent SIGTERM, exits with code -15 and LEAVES the PID file (after SIGTERM exists: True). The same child sent SIGINT removes the file (rc 0) because Python's default SIGINT handler raises KeyboardInterrupt which unwinds normally and runs atexit. So the documented promise is FALSE for SIGTERM/SIGKILL/crash and only TRUE for normal exit + SIGINT. SIGTERM is exactly how kill(1), launchd, and OS shutdown terminate a detached daemon (the watcher is spawned with start_new_session=True in the generated autolaunch script), so this is the most common real termination path.

RECYCLED-PID WEDGE: Confirmed by reading the guard. Both pid_is_running (watch_daemon.py:57-69) and the generated _pid_is_running (script body lines 178-198) only call os.kill(pid, 0): ProcessLookupError -> not running, PermissionError -> running(True), success -> True. There is NO identity check (no start-time, cmdline, or token). _existing_watcher_running() reads the stale PID; if that PID was recycled to any live process the user can signal, the guard returns True and main() (line 201-203) returns early, so the watcher is never restarted until a future iTerm2 launch happens to find the PID dead/unused.

PRIOR ROUNDS: Existing tests (tests/test_watch_daemon.py:424-450) cover only the happy path (PID written at startup / no-op without env, labeled Finding R2-watch #5 = the feature itself). No test or code addresses SIGTERM cleanup or recycled-PID. Not fixed in rounds 1-3.

SEVERITY ADJUSTMENT: Downgrading HIGH->MEDIUM. The stale-PID-file leak on SIGTERM is deterministic and the documentation is genuinely false, but the 'permanent wedge' consequence requires the dead PID to be recycled to a coincidentally-live same-user process AND that the autolaunch re-check only fires on iTerm2 launch — a probabilistic, self-healing-on-next-launch condition rather than guaranteed data loss or security impact. It is a real correctness/maintainability bug worth fixing (proposed SIGTERM/SIGHUP handler plus an identity token is the correct remedy), but not data-loss/security CRITICAL/HIGH severity. The claim's evidence is accurate, not fabricated.

title: Abandoned iterm2 worker threads accumulate (fd + connection leak) on apply timeout, and can apply a stale out-of-order mode after the watch loop moved on
description: run_iterm_bounded runs iterm2.run_until_complete on a daemon thread and joins with `timeout`. On timeout (worker.is_alive()) it raises RuntimeError and abandons the thread (iterm_connection.py:70-71). The event-loop close + fd reclamation lives in the worker's `finally` (lines 53-64) which only executes when the worker FINISHES — an abandoned, still-hung worker never reaches it, so its asyncio selector fd and open iterm2 websocket are NOT reclaimed. Under mode thrashing against a flaky/slow iTerm2 daemon, the watch loop (which catches RuntimeError and continues, watch_live.py:225-227) issues a new apply every cooldown, and each timed-out apply leaks one live thread. Reproduction: 10 calls each timing out at 0.2s -> 10 abandoned threads still alive (confirmed). Two harms: (1) thread/fd accumulation over a multi-year daemon; (2) an abandoned worker that later succeeds will call session.async_set_profile_properties applying a mode the loop already superseded, producing an out-of-order/stale apply (mode flicker) precisely when thrashing is most likely.
fix: Track in-flight apply threads and refuse to launch a new apply while a prior one is still alive (serialize applies), or cancel the prior coroutine/connection on timeout. At minimum, cap concurrent abandoned workers and surface a degraded state instead of unbounded leakage; consider closing the connection from the caller side on timeout so a late-completing worker cannot mutate the session.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against real code in src/term_chameleon/iterm_connection.py:44-71 and live_iterm.py. Both structural facts are confirmed:

1. THREAD/FD LEAK: run_iterm_bounded runs the worker on a daemon thread joined with timeout. The event-loop close + fd reclamation lives ONLY in the worker's finally (lines 53-64), which executes solely when the worker FINISHES. On timeout (line 70 worker.is_alive()) the thread is abandoned and never reaches finally, so its asyncio selector + self-pipe fds and any open iterm2 websocket are NOT reclaimed. Reproduced via .venv/bin/python: 10 calls each timing out at 0.2s left exactly 10 abandoned daemon threads alive (threads delta = 10). The docstring itself admits "On timeout the worker thread is abandoned."

2. STALE OUT-OF-ORDER APPLY: Reproduced a worker that times out at 0.20s but completes its work at 0.51s — i.e. after the timeout was reported and the watch loop (watch_live.py:223-231) caught RuntimeError, continued, and set next_allowed_switch. Since the real coroutine's async_set_profile_properties (live_iterm.py:71) is inside that worker, a late-completing abandoned worker mutates the session AFTER the loop superseded that decision -> mode flicker / out-of-order apply. No serialization, lock, in-flight tracking, or coroutine/connection cancellation exists anywhere (grep confirmed none).

isNew=true: The round-2 fix (commit 9133840) introduced run_iterm_bounded specifically to fix the fd leak, but only for the COMPLETION path; its verification ("0 fd growth across 40 applies") measured completing applies, not abandoned-on-timeout workers. The timeout-abandonment leak and the stale-apply race are not addressed in rounds 1-3.

Severity adjusted to MEDIUM (matches claim). The default apply timeout is 15s and applies normally complete in milliseconds, so the harm requires a genuinely wedged/slow iTerm2 daemon that repeatedly hangs >15s while the watch loop keeps thrashing modes — a real but uncommon condition. The proposed fix (serialize applies / refuse new apply while prior worker alive, or cancel on timeout) is sound. Not CRITICAL/HIGH because it does not corrupt data or break normal operation; it degrades a daemon under a flaky-daemon edge case.

title: No uninstall path for the installed Dynamic Profile or the make-default AutoLaunch script; install leaves permanent state with no removal command
description: The only uninstall subcommand is `uninstall-watch-daemon` (cli.py:161, handler cli.py:453). There is NO command to remove the Dynamic Profile written by `install`/`setup` (install.py:install_profile -> ~/Library/Application Support/iTerm2/DynamicProfiles/term-chameleon-adaptive-glass.json) nor the make-default AutoLaunch script (install.py:install_autolaunch_script -> .../Scripts/AutoLaunch/term_chameleon_default_profile.py). Reproduction: `term-chameleon install --make-default` writes a profile JSON and an AutoLaunch .py; nothing in the CLI (only `uninstall-watch-daemon` exists) can remove either. The profile and the AutoLaunch script that forces it default persist forever. install->uninstall therefore does NOT restore prior state: the Dynamic Profile, the make-default AutoLaunch script, and accumulated `.backup.*` siblings all leak. Confirmed: `grep add_parser cli.py` shows no profile-uninstall parser; the install handler (_install, cli.py:667-697) has no inverse.
fix: Add an `uninstall` (or `uninstall-profile`) subcommand that removes the Dynamic Profile JSON and the make-default AutoLaunch script (with a backup like the watch-daemon uninstall), mirroring uninstall_watch_autolaunch_script. Document the full uninstall sequence in the README alongside the existing watch-daemon uninstall guidance.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against actual source. cli.py:79-411 enumerates all subparsers: the only uninstall command is `uninstall-watch-daemon` (cli.py:161-172, handler cli.py:453-459 -> _uninstall_watch_daemon at 847-871, which calls uninstall_watch_autolaunch_script targeting only the watch daemon script). The `install` subcommand (cli.py:90-96) with `--make-default` is handled by _install (cli.py:667-697): it writes the Dynamic Profile JSON (install.py:40 filename "term-chameleon-adaptive-glass.json") and, when --make-default is set, the AutoLaunch script (install.py:15 AUTOLAUNCH_FILENAME "term_chameleon_default_profile.py"). install.py defines only install_profile, install_balanced_profile, install_autolaunch_script, autolaunch_script, profile_document — no removal/inverse function. `grep -rn uninstall src/ | grep -iv watch` returns nothing (exit 1). README only documents uninstall-watch-daemon. So the core claim is factually accurate: there is no command to remove the installed Dynamic Profile or the make-default AutoLaunch script.

Severity adjustment: This is a real lifecycle/usability gap, not a correctness or security defect. The leaked artifacts are static plain-text files in user-writable, documented iTerm2 directories (DynamicProfiles + Scripts/AutoLaunch) that a user can delete with rm. They cause no data loss, crash, or wrong runtime behavior. Per the severity table this maps to MEDIUM (maintainability concern), not HIGH. One sub-claim is slightly inaccurate: the ".backup.* siblings accumulate" point applies to the watch-daemon uninstall (which does back up); the profile install path overwrites in place and does not create .backup.* siblings — immaterial to the core finding.

Not fixed in prior rounds: no profile-uninstall exists in current code, so isNew=true.

title: make-default permanently changes the iTerm2 default profile and is never restored (no record of the previous default)
description: The generated AutoLaunch script calls `await profile.async_make_default()` (install.py:87) on every iTerm2 launch, permanently overriding whatever profile the user had set as default. Nothing in the codebase records the user's prior default profile (grep for 'make_default|restore|Default Bookmark|previous default' across src returns only the install.py call site and the CLI flag plumbing). Even if the AutoLaunch script were removed, the iTerm2 default would remain set to the Term Chameleon profile from the last launch. There is no command to restore the original default. This is a non-restorable state mutation of the user's environment.
fix: Before calling async_make_default(), capture the current default profile GUID/name (PartialProfile query) and persist it (e.g. under ~/Library/Application Support/term-chameleon/). Provide an uninstall/restore path that re-sets the saved default and removes the AutoLaunch script. At minimum, document prominently that make-default is not auto-reversible.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against real code. install.py:87 calls `await profile.async_make_default()`, and the bundled iterm2 lib (.venv/.../iterm2/profile.py:6596) implements async_make_default as `await iterm2.rpc.async_set_default_profile(self.connection, self.guid)` — i.e. it persists the default-profile selection in iTerm2 preferences permanently. The generated AutoLaunch script `term_chameleon_default_profile.py` runs this on every iTerm2 launch.

The supporting claims hold: grep for async_make_default across src/ returns ONLY this one call site; there is no PartialProfile query of the existing default, no persisted prior-default GUID/name, and no restore code anywhere. The only uninstall command (`uninstall-watch-daemon` -> watch_daemon.uninstall_watch_autolaunch_script) targets a DIFFERENT AutoLaunch file (`term_chameleon_watch_live.py`); it neither removes the default-profile script nor restores the user's prior default. So the tool provides no automatic way to undo the override.

isNew = true: prior rounds 1-3 contain several AutoLaunch findings, but every one concerns the watch-daemon script (PID write race, unbounded .backup pruning, dry-run removed flag, param range validation). None raise the make-default permanent default-override / no-prior-default-record / no-restore issue. This is a distinct, previously unreported finding.

Two accuracy caveats that bear on severity: (1) The override is opt-in — it only fires when the user passes the `--make-default` flag (cli.py:95, default off, autolaunch script only written under `if make_default:` at cli.py:688). (2) 'Non-restorable' is overstated: iTerm2 stores the default profile in preferences, and the user can manually re-select their original default in iTerm2 Preferences > Profiles, and removing the AutoLaunch script stops re-application on future launches. So it is 'not auto-reversible by the tool,' not truly unrecoverable. It mutates persistent user-environment state with no tool-provided rollback and no record of the prior value — a genuine UX/maintainability defect — but it is not data loss, a security issue, or irrecoverable, and it requires explicit opt-in. That places it at MEDIUM rather than HIGH. The proposed fix (capture prior default GUID before overriding, persist it, and add a restore/uninstall path; at minimum document the irreversibility) is sound.

title: All [project.urls] point to the wrong GitHub owner (broken PyPI links)
description: Homepage/Repository/Issues/Changelog all use `https://github.com/bodhi/term-chameleon` (lines 26-29). `bodhi` is the local macOS home-directory/username, not the repo owner. The actual remote is `git@github.com:connectwithprakash/term-chameleon.git` (verified via `git remote -v`). On a published PyPI page these four sidebar links (Homepage, Repository, Issues, Changelog) would all 404 / point to a non-existent repo. README contains no github.com links so the error is isolated to packaging metadata.
fix: Replace `bodhi` with `connectwithprakash` in all four URLs in [project.urls], e.g. `Homepage = "https://github.com/connectwithprakash/term-chameleon"` and likewise for Repository, Issues, Changelog.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the file and the git remote. pyproject.toml lines 26-29 hardcode the GitHub owner as `bodhi` for Homepage, Repository, Issues, and Changelog: e.g. `Homepage = "https://github.com/bodhi/term-chameleon"`. The actual remote is `git@github.com:connectwithprakash/term-chameleon.git` (verified via `git remote -v`). `bodhi` is the local macOS username (evident from the working directory `.`), not the repo owner. On a published PyPI page these four sidebar links would point to a repo under the wrong/non-existent owner and would not resolve to the canonical repo; the Changelog blob link would 404. `git log -S connectwithprakash -- pyproject.toml` returns nothing, confirming no commit (rounds 1-3 included) ever corrected the owner — the wrong value is still present at HEAD. The proposed fix is correct. Severity MEDIUM is apt: broken packaging metadata affecting discoverability/trust, no code or security or runtime impact, so not HIGH.

title: Concurrent read-modify-write of the same Dynamic Profile JSON silently loses updates (no locking around atomic_write_text)
description: ItermProfile.write / modes.apply_mode (modes.py:22-38) perform read (load_profile) -> modify in memory -> atomic_write_text. atomic_write_text guarantees the FILE is never half-written (tmp + os.replace is atomic), but provides NO mutual exclusion across the read-modify-write span. If two operations on the same Dynamic Profile path interleave (two `apply-mode` invocations, or `setup`/`install` racing a manual `apply`, or `fixes.py:83-84` overlapping), both read the same baseline, each mutates a different key, and the second writer's os.replace clobbers the first writer's change entirely. Reproduction with a barrier forcing both reads before either write: final state shows {A:1, B:0} when both A and B should be 1 -> LOST UPDATE (confirmed). atomic_write_text being 'atomic' masks the absence of inter-process serialization.
fix: Serialize read-modify-write on a per-file lock (fcntl.flock on the target or a sibling .lock) so the read and the write happen under one held lock, or adopt an optimistic-concurrency check (re-read mtime/hash before os.replace and retry on mismatch). Document that atomic_write_text protects against torn writes only, not lost updates.

verdict.reasoning:
Mechanism confirmed in source and reproduced. iterm_profile.py:107-111 (ItermProfile.write), modes.py:22-38 (apply_mode), and fixes.py:74-85 (fix_file) all perform read (load_profile) -> in-memory mutate -> atomic_write_text with no locking. safe_io.py:47-63 uses tempfile.mkstemp + Path.replace, which is atomic against torn writes but provides no mutual exclusion across the read-modify-write span. A barrier-based repro via the actual functions (load_profile + atomic_write_text on a real Dynamic Profile JSON) produced final state {A:1, B:0} when both A and B should be 1 -> lost update, exactly as the claim states. No fabricated evidence.

However, the MEDIUM severity overstates real-world impact for this single-user CLI. Adversarial check of the concurrency surface: (1) the watch daemon (watch_daemon.py) has a singleton PID guard via _existing_watcher_running, and critically applies changes through the live iTerm2 websocket API (apply_preset_to_current_session -> async_set_profile_properties, live_iterm.py:71), NOT by rewriting the profile JSON — so the most plausible "daemon vs manual apply" race against the same JSON file does not actually exist in the code. (2) The only genuine trigger is a user manually launching two write-commands against the same profile path within the same few-ms RMW window — a rare interactive race. (3) The failure mode is benign and recoverable: atomic_write_text still yields a valid file, a timestamped backup is taken first (backup_file), and the dropped change is just a re-runnable, idempotent appearance update. No persistent corruption or unrecoverable data loss. This is a correctly-diagnosed but low-impact robustness gap, better rated LOW than MEDIUM.

title: atomic_write_text leaves orphaned .tmp files in the target dir on SIGTERM mid-write
description: atomic_write_text creates a tempfile via mkstemp in target.parent and only unlinks it in the `except` clause. A SIGTERM (or SIGKILL) delivered between mkstemp (line 50) and tmp.replace (line 59) terminates the process without raising a Python exception, so the except cleanup never runs and the `.<name>.<rand>.tmp` file is orphaned in the profile/AutoLaunch directory. The target file itself stays intact (good — the rename is atomic). Over a multi-year watch daemon subjected to repeated kill/restart cycles, these `.tmp` files accumulate in ~/Library/Application Support/iTerm2/DynamicProfiles (and the AutoLaunch dir). Reproduction: fork child, slow the replace, SIGTERM it -> target intact but one orphaned `.profile.json.*.tmp` remains (confirmed).
fix: On daemon startup (or in install/apply paths), best-effort sweep stale `.<name>.*.tmp` files older than a threshold from target dirs. Optionally write tmp files to a dedicated subdir that is cleaned on startup, keeping the visible profile directory free of debris.

verdict.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL, LOW severity, NEW.

Code matches claim exactly (src/term_chameleon/safe_io.py:47-63). atomic_write_text calls tempfile.mkstemp (line 50) in target.parent, then writes/fsyncs and tmp.replace(target) at line 59. Cleanup (tmp.unlink) lives only in the `except Exception` block (lines 60-62), which a SIGTERM/SIGKILL bypasses entirely.

Reproduced with the project venv: monkeypatched mkstemp to sleep 30s between fd creation and return, forked the child, sent SIGTERM mid-window. Result: orphaned `.profile.json.ezbofht_.tmp` remained in the dir AND the target profile.json was never created (atomicity preserved). This confirms both halves of the claim: orphan left behind, target intact.

No mitigation exists in the codebase. grep across src/ and tests/ shows no tmp-sweep, no stale-file cleanup, and no signal handler in watch_daemon.py (which is the long-lived daemon and the only place where repeated kill/restart cycles realistically occur). The watch_daemon has PID-file staleness handling but nothing for orphaned write tmp files. The existing test test_atomic_write_text_cleans_up_temp_on_failure only covers the os.fdopen exception path, not signal termination — so this is genuinely NOT addressed in rounds 1-3 (round-3 commit c4b2e3d and round-1 af8a318 touched IO but not this).

Severity = LOW is correct and the claim self-assesses as LOW. Reasons it is not higher: (1) no data loss/corruption — atomic rename guarantees target integrity, explicitly acknowledged; (2) the vulnerable window is microscopic (write a few-KB JSON string + best-effort fsync), so the per-kill probability of orphaning is very small; (3) impact is pure disk hygiene — small hidden files in DynamicProfiles/AutoLaunch dirs. The "multi-year accumulation" framing is plausible but somewhat speculative since most kills miss the window. It is a legitimate, reproducible housekeeping gap worth a best-effort startup sweep, but nothing approaching MEDIUM.

title: All generated profiles share one hardcoded GUID, so two installs with different --name collide as duplicate iTerm2 Dynamic Profiles
description: profile_document() hardcodes `guid = guid or "TERM-CHAMELEON-ADAPTIVE-GLASS"` (install.py:21) regardless of the profile name or filename. Reproduction (run): profile_document(name='Adaptive Glass') and profile_document(name='Custom Name') both yield Guid='TERM-CHAMELEON-ADAPTIVE-GLASS'. iTerm2 Dynamic Profiles are keyed by GUID, so installing under two different --name values (or two filenames) produces two on-disk JSON files that iTerm2 sees as the SAME profile GUID — it logs a duplicate-GUID conflict and nondeterministically keeps one. State leaks across installs: a second install with a different filename does not replace the first file on disk (install_profile only backs up/overwrites the same filename), leaving an orphan JSON that conflicts in iTerm2.
fix: Derive the GUID deterministically from the profile name (e.g. a stable hash of name) or generate/persist a unique GUID per installed file, so distinct profiles do not collide and re-installs of the same logical profile remain stable.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against real code at src/term_chameleon/install.py:21. The GUID is hardcoded `guid = guid or "TERM-CHAMELEON-ADAPTIVE-GLASS"` and never derived from name/filename. Reproduced via the .venv python: profile_document(name='Adaptive Glass') and profile_document(name='Custom Name') both yield Guid='TERM-CHAMELEON-ADAPTIVE-GLASS' (collide:True). iTerm2 keying Dynamic Profiles by GUID is accurate, so two distinct on-disk files sharing one GUID is a genuine conflict.

HOWEVER the claim's headline framing is materially overstated. The CLI install command (cli.py:90-96, handler _install at 667-697) exposes --name but NOT --filename; install_profile always uses the default filename 'term-chameleon-adaptive-glass.json' from the CLI. I tested both scenarios on disk in /tmp: two installs with different --name values produce exactly ONE file (the second backs up and overwrites the first via backup_file + atomic_write_text) — no orphan, no on-disk duplicate, no iTerm2 conflict. The "two on-disk JSON files that iTerm2 sees as the SAME profile GUID" outcome ONLY occurs when passing distinct filename= values, which is reachable solely via the internal Python API (install_profile(filename=...)). That parameter is untested, unused internally, and not user-reachable through the CLI.

So: the design smell (shared hardcoded GUID defeating per-profile identity / breaking idempotency if anyone builds multi-profile installs on the API) is real and never fixed (git -S shows the line unchanged since the original scaffold commit 39b8420, confirming isNew). But the claimed user-facing duplicate/orphan-conflict via --name does not occur — different --name alone overwrites a single file. Practical impact is confined to an internal API edge that the product never exercises, so severity is LOW rather than MEDIUM. The proposed fix (deterministic GUID from name) is still reasonable hygiene.

title: Identical re-installs unconditionally create a backup of byte-identical content, leaking redundant backup files
description: install_profile (install.py:48-49) and install_autolaunch_script (install.py:106-108) call backup_file() whenever the target exists, with no content comparison. Reproduction (run): installing the same profile three times leaves term-chameleon-adaptive-glass.json plus two `.backup.<timestamp>` files in the DynamicProfiles dir even though every write was byte-identical. While safe_io._prune_backups now caps total backups at MAX_BACKUPS=5 (so growth is bounded), re-running an idempotent install still churns backups of unchanged files and pushes out genuinely older backups. uninstall-watch-daemon similarly adds yet another backup on removal, none of which are cleaned by any uninstall.
fix: Skip backup_file()/atomic_write when the new content equals the existing file content (read-and-compare before writing), making re-install truly idempotent and avoiding redundant backups.

verdict.reasoning: Verified by reading code and reproducing. install_profile (install.py:48-49) and install_autolaunch_script (install.py:106-107) call backup_file() whenever target.exists(), with no content comparison before atomic_write_text. Reproduced: running install_profile three times into /tmp/tc_test left term-chameleon-adaptive-glass.json plus exactly 2 .backup.<timestamp> files, all three byte-identical (same SHA-1 ba634f1...). So idempotent re-installs do churn redundant backups of unchanged content. The same unconditional-backup pattern also exists in watch_daemon.py:239 and :284 (uninstall_watch_autolaunch_script backs up on removal), modes.py:37, and fixes.py:83. The claim's caveats are accurate: safe_io._prune_backups caps backups at MAX_BACKUPS=5 (safe_io.py:12,26-34), so growth is bounded, not an unbounded leak — but churn still pushes out genuinely older backups, and no uninstall cleans backups. The proposed read-and-compare-before-write fix is sound. Not fixed in rounds 1-3: current code still has no content comparison; the MAX_BACKUPS cap (referenced in the claim itself) was the prior mitigation, not a fix for the redundant-backup behavior. Severity LOW is appropriate — minor wastefulness / imperfect idempotency, no correctness or security impact, bounded disk usage.

title: Dead [visual] optional dependency: pillow declared but never imported
description: pyproject declares an optional dependency `visual = ["pillow>=10.0"]` (line 34), implying screenshot/visual commands need Pillow. In reality nothing in the package imports PIL/Pillow: `grep -rln "PIL|pillow|Pillow" src/ tests/` returns nothing. The package ships its own PNG codec (src/term_chameleon/png.py uses struct+zlib) and raster handling (images.py). I confirmed all visual-path commands run in the .venv (which has no Pillow installed): `visual-test`, `background-html`, `pattern-script`, `e2e-stage`, `screenshot-test`, `sample` all exit 0. The `[visual]` extra is therefore entirely dead — installing `term-chameleon[visual]` pulls a ~10MB dependency that is never used. It is also referenced nowhere in README, CHANGELOG, docs/, or CI (CI only uses `--extra dev`).
fix: Remove the `visual = ["pillow>=10.0"]` extra from [project.optional-dependencies] in pyproject.toml, OR, if Pillow-backed image handling is intended future work, document it and gate it behind an actual import. As shipped it is misleading dead metadata.

verdict.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. pyproject.toml:34 declares `visual = ["pillow>=10.0"]` under [project.optional-dependencies]. A word-boundary grep for `\bPIL\b|pillow` across src/ and tests/ returns nothing (the prior grep hits were all the `compile` builtin, not pillow). The .venv has no PIL installed (ModuleNotFoundError on `import PIL`), yet all visual modules (visual.py, png.py, images.py, screenshot_test.py, e2e_stage.py, background_html.py, terminal_pattern.py) import cleanly and `term-chameleon visual-test <profile>` and `e2e-stage <profile>` run to exit 0 with PIL absent from sys.modules. The package ships its own PNG codec (png.py via struct+zlib) and raster handling (images.py), so it genuinely has no need for Pillow. No README/CHANGELOG/docs/CI reference to the visual extra or pillow. So the [visual] extra is genuinely dead, misleading metadata.

One evidence imprecision in the claim: it states the listed visual commands `exit 0` with no arguments. They actually require a `profile` positional and exit 2 (argparse usage error) when invoked bare, as I reproduced (`visual-test exit: 2`, `e2e-stage exit: 2`). With the required profile arg they exit 0. This does not change the core finding (Pillow is never imported), but the cited reproduction detail is inaccurate.

Severity: this is inert packaging metadata. No runtime bug, no security/data-loss impact. Worst case a user installs an unused dependency (Pillow wheel is ~3-4MB, not the ~10MB claimed). That is a maintainability/correctness-of-metadata nit, which maps to LOW, not MEDIUM. Adjusting down.

isNew: true. Grep of all three prior audit JSON files (rounds 1-3) for `pillow` returned zero matches, so this was not previously reported.

title: README brands v0.1.1 as 'beta' while package metadata and CHANGELOG declare it stable
description: The current README (package version 0.1.1) repeatedly calls the release a beta: line 9 'a dogfooded beta for...', line 47 'Build and install the beta wheel locally', line 58 'dogfooded for this beta'. This contradicts the actual release state: pyproject.toml line 15 sets `Development Status :: 5 - Production/Stable`, and CHANGELOG.md line 24 (0.1.0) states 'Promoted from beta to stable after sustained daemon dogfood'. 0.1.1 is built on top of the stable 0.1.0 (both dated 2026-06-27). So the headline status the user reads in the README directly conflicts with the PyPI classifier and the changelog — a reader cannot tell whether this is beta or stable.
fix: Update README 'Current status' and 'Beta release verification' sections to reflect the stable status (or, if it really should be beta, revert the pyproject classifier to `4 - Beta` and the CHANGELOG 0.1.0 'promoted to stable' note). Pick one source of truth and make README/pyproject/CHANGELOG agree.

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly against the literal file contents. README.md brands v0.1.1 as a beta in four places: line 9 ("a dogfooded beta for..."), line 45 (heading "## Beta release verification"), line 47 ("Build and install the beta wheel locally"), and line 58 ("dogfooded for this beta"). This contradicts pyproject.toml line 15 (`"Development Status :: 5 - Production/Stable"`) and CHANGELOG.md 0.1.0 (lines 22-32: "### Stable release", "Promoted from beta to stable after sustained daemon dogfood", "Promoted classifier to Development Status :: 5 - Production/Stable"). Both 0.1.0 and 0.1.1 are dated 2026-06-27 and 0.1.1 builds on stable 0.1.0. Git history confirms the root cause: at the 0.1.0 stable release commit (3965a56) the version was bumped from 0.1.0b4 to 0.1.0 and the classifier/CHANGELOG were promoted to stable, but the README "beta" wording was never updated — the 0.1.1 release commit (2aa556c) only bumped version numbers and left all four "beta" references intact. So the README a user reads first directly conflicts with the PyPI classifier and changelog. The claim's cited evidence (specific line numbers and file contents) all reproduce exactly. This is a documentation-only inconsistency with no runtime, security, or functional impact — purely reader/user confusion about release maturity. The proposed fix (pick one source of truth and align README/pyproject/CHANGELOG) is correct. Severity adjusted from MEDIUM to LOW because the impact is confined to docs wording with no behavioral consequence; MEDIUM would be defensible on user-trust grounds but the realistic harm is minor.

title: CHANGELOG claim 'help= text to all key CLI arguments' is overstated; several documented arguments have no help
description: CHANGELOG 0.1.1 line 19 (and 0.1.0 line 30) claim 'Added `help=` text to all key CLI arguments.' Inspecting actual --help output shows multiple arguments with empty help text, including ones used in README examples: `install --name` and `install --target-dir` (used in README line 102) render with no description; `release-check --threshold` (used in README lines 55/68) has no help; `release-check --profile/--width/--height/--settle-delay` have none; and the `osc` subcommand's two positionals (`{apply,reset}` and the preset choice) have no help even though `osc apply --write` is a headline 0.1.1 feature (CHANGELOG line 8). Reproduction: `.venv/bin/python -m term_chameleon.cli install --help` and `... release-check --help` show the bare `--name NAME` / `--threshold THRESHOLD` lines with no following description.
fix: Either add help= strings to these arguments (especially --name, --target-dir, --threshold, and the osc positionals, which appear in README examples) or soften the CHANGELOG wording from 'all key CLI arguments' to reflect that coverage is partial.

verdict.reasoning: Reproduced directly. `term-chameleon install --help` shows `--name NAME` and `--target-dir TARGET_DIR` with no description; both appear in README line 102 (`install --target-dir ... --name ...`). `release-check --help` shows `--threshold THRESHOLD`, `--profile`, `--width`, `--height`, `--settle-delay` with no help, while `--threshold` is used in README lines 55 and 68. `osc --help` shows both positionals (`{apply,reset}` and the preset choice) with no help, yet `osc apply --write` is a headline 0.1.1 feature (CHANGELOG line 8). CHANGELOG lines 19 and 30 both state 'Added `help=` text to all key CLI arguments.' Given arguments used in README examples and a headline feature lack help, the 'all key CLI arguments' phrasing is overstated. Not a bug in code behavior — a documentation accuracy issue, correctly rated LOW. The hedge word 'key' gives partial cover but arguments in documented examples reasonably count as key. Verified this is the current repo state (HEAD at 2aa556c, v0.1.1), so not already fixed.

title: Headline feature osc apply --write and commands terminal-info / watch-sim have no runnable example in README
description: The 'CLI examples' section documents 28 commands but omits three that the docs otherwise tout. `osc apply --write` is a headline 0.1.1 CHANGELOG entry (line 8: 'writes raw OSC escape sequences to any OSC-capable terminal') and OSC generation is in the README Implemented list (line 18), yet the `osc` command appears in zero README example blocks. `terminal-info` is referenced in prose (README line 35) but has no example. `watch-sim` is in the Implemented list (line 19) with no example. All three exist and work: `osc apply balanced --write`, `terminal-info`, and `watch-sim` are real subcommands (verified via --help and execution). A reader cannot discover the syntax for the cross-terminal OSC feature that the changelog highlights.
fix: Add example invocations for `osc apply <preset> [--write|--tmux|--shell]`, `osc reset`, `terminal-info`, and `watch-sim` to the README CLI examples section so the documented headline cross-terminal feature is actually demonstrated.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified every element of the claim against real source.

1. Commands exist and run: `osc apply <preset> [--write|--tmux|--shell]`, `osc reset`, `terminal-info`, and `watch-sim` are all real subcommands. Confirmed via `--help` and live execution (.venv/bin/term-chameleon). `osc apply balanced` emits the documented OSC 10/11 sequences; `--write` is a real flag ("Write raw escape sequences to stdout"); `terminal-info` prints terminal detection; `watch-sim` runs the mode simulator.

2. The headline/prose framing is accurate: CHANGELOG.md:8 touts `osc apply --write` ("writes raw OSC escape sequences to any OSC-capable terminal"); README:18 lists OSC generation, README:19 lists `watch-sim`, README:35 references `terminal-info` plus OSC application as a cross-terminal feature.

3. The gap is real: `grep -nE "term-chameleon (osc|terminal-info|watch-sim)" README.md` returns ZERO matches. The CLI examples section (README:75-194) documents ~28 commands across many code blocks (check, release-check, setup, install, mode, iterm-*, screenshot-*, sample, adapt-once, watch-live, doctor, fix, etc.) but contains no runnable example for the three commands the docs otherwise highlight. A reader cannot discover the syntax for the v0.1.1 headline cross-terminal OSC feature from the examples.

This is genuinely a documentation completeness issue, correctly rated LOW (no functional/security/correctness impact; commands work). Confidence high because every factual assertion in the claim reproduced exactly.
